title: Why the corner where two streets meet matters more than the streets
summary: Argue that the intersections of a city — corners, junctions, small squares — do more for civic life than the streets that lead into them, drawing on a specific example.
topic: Why street corners matter more to civic life than the streets themselves
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing that street corners and small intersections do more for the social life of a city than the streets that lead into them. Use a specific example. Use the present perfect for observation, second conditional, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: On the corner of Rue Mercière and a small unnamed alley in the second arrondissement of Lyon, every weekday between five and seven, something happens that the long straight streets on either side never quite manage to produce…
If you removed the corner café, the bench, and the slight widening of the pavement, what would remain on the same map would technically still be a neighbourhood, but…
Although planners almost always design with streets in mind, what residents actually use to organise their social life is something smaller and more specific, which is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: dừng lại ở một góc phố
đời sống công dân, đời sống cộng đồng
điểm gặp gỡ không chính thức

sampleText:
On the corner of Rue Mercière and a small unnamed alley in the second arrondissement of Lyon, every weekday between five and seven, something happens that the long straight streets on either side never quite manage to produce. People who live within ten minutes' walk drift toward that corner without planning to. They stop at the small café whose tables spill out onto a slightly widened pavement; they read the printed notices pinned on the wall of the boulangerie next door; they greet a friend they had not arranged to meet; they linger long enough to notice who else is around. The streets that lead into this corner are busy enough during the day, but no one stops on them. Stopping happens at the corner, and stopping is what turns a piece of pavement into something more than a route.

What I have come to think, after watching corners like this in several cities, is that the social life of a neighbourhood is not really produced by streets — it is produced by the small interruptions where streets meet. A street invites you to keep moving; a corner invites you to pause. The pause is everything. If two people who would otherwise walk past each other both stop for a moment at a corner, a greeting becomes possible. If they only meet for a second on a long straight pavement, the geometry pushes them apart almost before they have recognised one another. Although a city can have beautiful streets and still feel socially empty, I have rarely seen a city full of generous, well-formed corners that did not feel alive at street level. The corner is where the city becomes a place where people happen to be in the same spot at the same moment, which is the basic condition of community.

However, this is something most urban planning continues to ignore. New developments are designed around streets, road widths, and through-traffic; corners are treated as accidents of where two streets happen to meet, not as the actual product the design should aim to create. If a city wanted to make its neighbourhoods more sociable without changing very much, it could simply work backwards from the corner — slightly widen the pavement at every intersection, place a bench, allow one small café or kiosk to take outdoor space, plant a single tree for shade. None of this requires major investment. What it requires is a different mental picture of what a city is for. The streets get you from one place to another. The corners are the places. A neighbourhood with weak corners is a neighbourhood made of roads, even when the roads themselves are pleasant — and a neighbourhood made of roads is much harder to love than one made of small, well-shaped pauses.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đời sống xã hội của một khu phố không thực sự được tạo ra bởi các con phố

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional with 'otherwise' for hypothetical comparison
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If two people who would otherwise walk past each other both stop for a moment at a corner' dùng điều kiện loại 2 lồng với 'otherwise' (trong tình huống khác). 'Would + otherwise' diễn đạt điều sẽ xảy ra nếu hoàn cảnh khác. Cấu trúc này rất hữu ích cho lập luận giả định.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If two people who would otherwise walk past each other both stop for a moment at a corner' embeds 'otherwise' (in another scenario) in a second conditional. 'Would + otherwise' expresses what would happen under different circumstances. This structure is very useful for hypothetical argument.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence with 'what … is' to emphasise a definition
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Stopping is what turns a piece of pavement into something more than a route' và 'What it requires is a different mental picture of what a city is for' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'X is what Y' / 'What X is Y' để nhấn mạnh phần được định nghĩa. Đây là cấu trúc mạnh trong văn nghị luận.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Stopping is what turns a piece of pavement into something more than a route' and 'What it requires is a different mental picture of what a city is for' use the cleft structure 'X is what Y' / 'What X is Y' to emphasise a defined element. This is a powerful structure in argumentative writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ở góc đường Rue Mercière và một con hẻm nhỏ không tên ở quận hai của Lyon, mỗi ngày trong tuần từ năm đến bảy giờ, điều gì đó xảy ra mà những con phố thẳng dài ở hai bên không bao giờ thực sự tạo ra được. Người sống trong vòng đi bộ mười phút trôi dạt về phía góc đó mà không có kế hoạch. Họ dừng ở quán cà phê nhỏ có những bàn tràn ra vỉa hè được mở rộng nhẹ; họ đọc các thông báo in được ghim trên tường của tiệm bánh kế bên; họ chào một người bạn họ đã không hẹn gặp; họ nán lại đủ lâu để nhận ra ai khác đang ở quanh đó. Những con phố dẫn vào góc này đủ tấp nập trong ngày, nhưng không ai dừng lại trên chúng. Việc dừng xảy ra ở góc, và việc dừng lại là cái biến một mảng vỉa hè thành thứ gì đó hơn là một tuyến đường.

Điều tôi đã dần nghĩ, sau khi quan sát các góc phố như thế này ở vài thành phố, là đời sống xã hội của một khu phố không thực sự được tạo ra bởi các con phố — nó được tạo ra bởi những sự gián đoạn nhỏ nơi các con phố gặp nhau. Một con phố mời bạn tiếp tục đi; một góc phố mời bạn dừng lại. Sự dừng lại là tất cả. Nếu hai người sẽ đi qua nhau cùng dừng lại một khoảnh khắc ở góc, một lời chào trở nên có thể. Nếu họ chỉ gặp nhau trong một giây trên một vỉa hè thẳng dài, hình học đẩy họ tách ra gần như trước khi họ đã nhận ra nhau. Mặc dù một thành phố có thể có những con phố đẹp mà vẫn cảm thấy trống rỗng về mặt xã hội, tôi hiếm khi thấy một thành phố đầy những góc phố rộng rãi, được tạo hình tốt mà không cảm thấy sống động ở cấp đường phố. Góc phố là nơi thành phố trở thành nơi mà người ta tình cờ ở cùng một chỗ vào cùng một khoảnh khắc, đó là điều kiện cơ bản của cộng đồng.

Tuy nhiên, đây là điều mà hầu hết quy hoạch đô thị tiếp tục bỏ qua. Các dự án phát triển mới được thiết kế xung quanh các con phố, độ rộng đường, và giao thông xuyên suốt; các góc phố được coi như những ngẫu nhiên của nơi hai con phố tình cờ gặp nhau, không phải là sản phẩm thực sự mà thiết kế nên nhắm tới. Nếu một thành phố muốn làm cho các khu phố của mình thân thiện hơn về mặt xã hội mà không thay đổi nhiều, nó có thể đơn giản đi ngược từ góc phố — mở rộng nhẹ vỉa hè ở mỗi giao lộ, đặt một chiếc ghế, cho phép một quán cà phê nhỏ hoặc quầy bán hàng chiếm không gian ngoài trời, trồng một cái cây để lấy bóng mát. Không gì trong số này đòi hỏi đầu tư lớn. Điều nó đòi hỏi là một bức tranh tinh thần khác về việc một thành phố để làm gì. Các con phố đưa bạn từ nơi này đến nơi khác. Các góc phố là những nơi chốn. Một khu phố với những góc phố yếu là một khu phố làm bằng đường phố, ngay cả khi chính các con đường đó dễ chịu — và một khu phố làm bằng đường phố khó yêu hơn nhiều so với một khu phố làm bằng những khoảng dừng nhỏ, được tạo hình tốt.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the second conditional with 'would otherwise' correctly?
options: If two people who would otherwise walked past each other both stop, a greeting becomes possible.
If two people who will otherwise walk past each other both stop, a greeting becomes possible.
If two people who would otherwise walk past each other both stop, a greeting becomes possible.
If two people who would otherwise walk past each other both stopped, a greeting will become possible.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would + base verb' for the hypothetical 'otherwise' scenario; the main if-clause uses present simple ('stop') and the main clause uses present simple ('becomes') for a general statement.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Would + base verb' inside the otherwise-clause; generic present elsewhere.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the cleft 'X is what Y' correctly?
options: Stopping is what turn a piece of pavement into something more than a route.
Stopping is that turns a piece of pavement into something more than a route.
Stopping is what to turn a piece of pavement into something more than a route.
Stopping is what turns a piece of pavement into something more than a route.
mcQuestions.explanation: Cleft 'X is what + verb'; the verb agrees with 'what' (treated as singular here), so 'turns'. 'That' is not used here; no 'to' after 'what'.

options: Although a city can have beautiful streets and still feel socially empty, I have rarely seen one with generous corners that felt empty.
Although a city can have beautiful streets and still feel socially empty, but I have rarely seen one with generous corners that felt empty.
Although, a city can have beautiful streets and still feel socially empty I have rarely seen one with generous corners that felt empty.
A city can have beautiful streets and still feel socially empty, although however I have rarely seen one with generous corners that felt empty.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'come to think' correctly?
options: What I came to think, after watching corners like this in several cities, is that social life is not produced by streets.
What I have come to think, after watching corners like this in several cities, is that social life is not produced by streets.
What I have came to think, after watching corners like this in several cities, is that social life is not produced by streets.
What I have come thinking, after watching corners like this in several cities, is that social life is not produced by streets.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a possessive relative clause correctly?
options: The small café which tables spill out onto a slightly widened pavement.
The small café that's tables spill out onto a slightly widened pavement.
The small café whose tables spill out onto a slightly widened pavement.
The small café whom tables spill out onto a slightly widened pavement.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whose' is the possessive relative pronoun, used for things as well as people; 'which' does not have a possessive form by itself; 'that's' is a contraction, not a relative pronoun.
mcQuestions.hint: Possessive relative pronoun = 'whose' (used for things and people).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'where + clause' correctly to define a place?
options: The places that two streets happen to meet.
The places in where two streets happen to meet.
The places which two streets happen to meet there.
The places where two streets happen to meet.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Where' is the relative adverb for places; 'that' and 'which' need a preposition; do not repeat 'there'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Where' = relative adverb for place; no extra preposition, no repeated 'there'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'one made of' correctly to refer back to a noun?
options: A neighbourhood made of roads is much harder to love than one made of small, well-shaped pauses.
A neighbourhood made of roads is much harder to love than ones made of small, well-shaped pauses.
A neighbourhood made of roads is much harder to love than that made of small, well-shaped pauses.
A neighbourhood made of roads is much harder to love than it made of small, well-shaped pauses.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'One' is the substitute pronoun replacing the singular noun 'neighbourhood'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'One' replaces a singular countable noun in this comparative structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'continues to ignore' correctly?
options: This is something most urban planning continues ignore.
This is something most urban planning continues to ignore.
This is something most urban planning continues ignoring it.
This is something most urban planning continues to ignored.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Continue to + base verb' is correct; 'continue + -ing' would also be possible but with no object pronoun added; 'to + past participle' is wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'happen to + base verb' correctly?
options: People happen being in the same spot at the same moment.
People happens to be in the same spot at the same moment.
People happen to be in the same spot at the same moment.
People happen to being in the same spot at the same moment.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Happen to + base verb' is the fixed structure; 'people' is plural, so 'happen' (no -s); base verb after 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Happen to + base verb'; plural subject takes no -s.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'much harder to + base verb' correctly?
options: A neighbourhood made of roads is much hard to love.
A neighbourhood made of roads is much harder for love.
A neighbourhood made of roads is much harder loving.
A neighbourhood made of roads is much harder to love.
mcQuestions.explanation: Comparative 'harder' (irregular comparison form) + 'to + base verb'; 'much' intensifies the comparative.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'work backwards from' correctly?
options: It could simply work backwards from the corner.
It could simply work backwards by the corner.
It could simply work backwards to the corner.
It could simply work backwards of the corner.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Work backwards from + starting point' is the standard collocation.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If most city planning still focuses on streets rather than corners, what is the simplest change a single resident could push for in their neighbourhood — and would small changes at one corner actually be enough to shift the social life of the surrounding streets?
